Based on these experiences, we have created the first version of the document in 2019 as an easy-to-follow, step-by-step guide. This guide outlined the migration process, explaining both the technical setup and how to ensure a smooth experience for users.

We’re excited to announce our updated Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guide based on SAP S/4HANA release 2023.

This new version provides more streamlined steps, updated tools and further recommendations to help you successfully move from standalone FES to embedded FES deployment.

Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Why do you need this guide?

This guide is essential for SAP S/4HANA customers who are looking to move from a standalone FES to an embedded FES deployment. It provides clear, step-by-step instructions to make the transition smooth, covering both technical and practical aspects of the migration.

Whether you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA system or aligning with SAP's latest architectural recommendations, this guide ensures you have everything you need i.e. from planning, installation, and configuration to post-migration testing and optimization. It helps you:

  • Understand the benefits of moving to embedded FES deployment.
  • Know the right time to make the shift.
  • Navigate technical prerequisites, sizing, and system considerations.
  • Simplify the migration process with actionable steps, tools, and examples.
  • Ensure a seamless experience for users by resolving common issues.
  • Minimize disruptions by retaining user personalization and role assignments.
  • Gain tips on how to enhance usability and performance

By following this guide, you can align with SAP best practices to unlock the full potential of SAP Fiori embedded FES deployment.

 

What's in it / What's new?

  • Enhance performance with tips for optimizing launchpad features, OData services, and app configurations

    • This includes reviewing and improving performance for SAP GUI Apps, SAP Fiori Apps, SAP Fiori Launchpad etc. and best practices to adjust and transport launchpad configuration parameters
  • Why and how to use Clean Core SAP Fiori administration tools to perform the migration
    • Clean core tools like Launchpad Content Manager, Launchpad Application Manager, Manage Launchpad Pages, Manage Launchpad Spaces provide enhanced capabilities compared to classical tools like Launchpad Designer. 
  • Structured 6-Step Content Migration
    • Comprehensive steps to migrate roles, catalogs, spaces and pages.
  • User Personalization Retention
    • Tools like /UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO to migrate homepages, private views, and UI changes.
  • Multi-Client Activation
    • Detailed guidance on enabling and configuring new clients post-migration.
  • Improved Testing and Troubleshooting
    • Expanded scenarios for UAT, error handling using App Support, and validation tools.
  • Performance and usability enhancements
    • Parameters for faster navigation, optimized search, and stateful SAP GUI containers.
  • Web Dispatcher Guidance
    • Steps to switch from Standalone to Embedded without changing end-user URLs.

 

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on our  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

Other helpful links:

  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: The Trace – Watching Object Dependencies at Work
entry.content:
Dependencies in SAP AVC (Advanced Variant Configuration) models can become quite complex and, thus, confusing. In this case, it’s beneficial to check what’s happening: The trace helps you track which dependencies are executed, when, and with which result. The user’s input and its effects are also visible here.

One caveat upfront: As helpful as the trace is to gain insights into the valuation process, it also impairs performance. So, when you want the dependency machinery to work fast, you better turn off the trace.

This blog post will help you familiarize yourself with the trace feature. After general remarks on its use, we outline an example to illustrate what’s inside the trace log. The important Trace Filters feature is discussed separately, followed by the option to navigate directly to dependencies or objects starting from a trace entry.

 

Use

The trace can be activated in the right pane next to the valuation screen of the simulation UI (the inspector panel). If you don’t see the inspector panel, use the “Toggle Inspector Panel” button at the top right.

 

 

 

Switch from Inspector to Trace and turn on the trace when you’re ready to trace the model’s dependencies.

Use the Search window to find trace entries in large trace logs quickly. The search only works with the technical names of the objects, so you should switch them on in the settings (cogwheel at the top right). The recommendation is to use “Both” (technical name and description).

The following example is supposed to give you an idea of how the trace works and how to read the entries.

 

Example: Check the Gripper

We want to test the model of a robot arm that can pick and drop things. Depending on the environment (“controlled” or “harsh”) and the objects to handle (characteristic “Delicate Parts Handling” can have values “Yes” or “No”), a suitable gripper has to be selected: The “Gripper Technology” can be “Mechanical Gripper” or “Vacuum Gripper.”

It is also possible to choose no value, which can’t be used for the gripper technology, as it is a mandatory characteristic. This is why “(None)” would result in an incomplete configuration.

Let’s turn on the trace and see what happens when we choose the value “Harsh Environment.” This choice triggers the execution of object dependencies because the values for Gripper Technology and Delicate Parts Handling have been determined (“Mechanical Gripper” and “No”), and the user can’t change them anymore.

The trace has a lot going on—68 entries have appeared, as we can see when scrolling down.

But we’re only interested in the entries starting with no. 8, where the user input is processed (our header material is RBT_VA_BUNDLE).

 

User Input

The details for position no. 9 show that the user assigned the value HARSH (the technical name for “Harsh Environment”) to the characteristic RBT_ENVIRONMENT of header material RBT_VA_BUNDLE.

The cogwheel symbols show us the values before the change (cogwheel with arrow pointing to the left) and after the change (arrow pointing to the right). Note that these symbols are only in place with high-level dependencies (as opposed to low-level dependencies for bill of materials (BOM) and routing explosion)! CTRLD is the technical name for “Controlled Environment.”

 

Constraint Execution, Part 1

In the next step, no. 10, the constraint named RBT_VAB_RESTRICT_GRIP_TECH is executed. The bifurcation symbol with the three connected tiny circles stands for “Object Dependency.” The number in round brackets indicates that the first restriction of the constraint is executed. Restrictions are separated by commas so you can see which part is addressed by the number. In our case, there are comments that number the restrictions sequentially.

According to the first restriction in the constraint, the value MECH is chosen by the constraint.

 

Constraint Execution, Part 2

Let’s have a closer look at the editor coding of the constraint. Scrolling down, we can see a second restriction:

This restriction determines that if fragile parts are handled (RBT_WP_FRAGILE = ‘YES’), the gripper must be of the vacuum type (VACM). As an implication, if RBT_GRIP_TECH does not have the value VACM, the characteristic RBT_WP_FRAGILE must have the value NO. This is because restrictions in constraints state general rules and enforce them like ‘laws of nature’ (if applicable, a condition part must be fulfilled upfront, which doesn’t exist here). When a restriction is formulated like “if A, then B,” it’s equivalent to the logical contrapositive “if not B, then not A.”

This being said, we expect the constraint to spring into action again: The characteristic RBT_GRIP_TECH for the grip technology has been set to MECH (i.e., not VACM) during the constraint's first execution, and therefore, RBT_WP_FRAGILE should be set to NO.

As you can tell from step 11 of the trace, this is precisely what happens:

The second restriction of the constraint is executed, picking the value NO for RBT_WP_FRAGILE because RBT_GRIP_TECH has been set to MECH.

 

Trace Filters

The Trace Filters option is a handy feature. You can access it with the funnel symbol button beside the search window.

The Trace Filters pop-up shows you five categories to find results in the trace log:

Trace Level

In the Trace Level, you can narrow your search to high-level or low-level configuration or leave it blank to see both. High-level dependencies are interactive dependencies, and low-level dependencies directly affect BOM and routing. Both can occur interleaved in multi-level models, e.g., when a sales order is validated.

Message Type

You can select one or more entries from this drop-down list. Several entries are connected by an OR logic, which means, e.g., the selection “Values Assignment” plus “Value Restriction” finds all entries where a value is assigned to a characteristic or the domain of values is restricted.

Value Assignment By

Values can be assigned by a user or automatically:

Characteristic

Here, you can enter the names of one or more characteristics using the F4 help.

Dependency

Choose one or more dependencies to look for.

 

Direct Navigation

In the tracing pane, you can directly select a material component, a characteristic, or a dependency. Clicking on an element gives you several options, such as filtering for it or inspecting it.

Let’s click on characteristic RBT_GRIP_TECH in trace entry no. 10, and then choose “Where this was changed”:

As a result, we get back entries no. 10 (again) and no. 61:

This possibility leads us to a best practice in case of an inconsistency: If an inconsistency occurs, search for the “Inconsistency Detected” message type. Then, take the characteristic that has become inconsistent and search upwards using “Where this was changed.”

entry.title: Service with Advanced Execution: Process Flow 4 – Service Quotation with Catalogue Price
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management.

The concept of Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution is introduced in the following blog:

Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution

Within Service with Advanced Execution, there are various process flows depending on the business scenario. These process flows are distinguished based on two aspects:

  • How the process starts, and which is the first transaction.
  • What is the trigger for the creation of the maintenance order and eventually the start of planning.

Process Flow 4:

This blog showcases the process flow wherein the initial transaction is the service order quotation, and the maintenance order is not created in the service order quotation phase.

For example, let us assume that a car dealership offers maintenance and repair services for various car models. A customer requests a standard service, such as an engine oil change or brake replacement, and asks for a quotation. The Service Manager has agreed with the customer to perform fixed price billing. The dealership maintains a price master that lists predefined prices for services and parts.

In such scenarios, the service manager will create a quotation to provide a cost estimate for the service requested by the customer. Since the cost of service is agreed upon with the help of existing price master, there is no need of a detailed planning through maintenance order.

Creation of Service Order Quotation

A service order quotation can be created using the ‘Create Service Quotation’ App.

The following details could be filled while creating a service order quotation document:

  • Sold-to-Party
  • Organizational data such as Sales Organization and optionally Service Organization
  • Parties involved such as Service Employee Group and Employee Responsible
  • Execution Order Item with Planning Plant, Work Center, DIP Profile and Billing Relevance
  • Reference Objects

After entering all the details, save the service order quotation document. The service order quotation gets created with an execution order item with lifecycle status as 'Open'.

Status lifecycle in Service with Advanced Execution

                                                           Current state of an execution order item

The state diagram illustrates the status lifecycle of one execution order item along with its corresponding maintenance order (if any) in this process flow. Each block below represents a state displaying 3 different status values. The lifecycle and execution status of an execution order item along with the status of the corresponding maintenance order.

 

Send to Customer

Once the cost estimate is derived for the requested service, either based on the price master or based on any manual intervention, the quotation can be sent to the customer for approval. When the service order quotation is sent to customer, the execution order item status is updated to 'Released'.

Accept and creation of Follow-up Service Order

When the customer accepts the service order quotation, its lifecycle is updated to 'Completed'. A follow-up service order is automatically created, carrying over the existing execution order item.

Further processing of the service order follows the regular process flow.

Summary

The distinct feature in this process flow is the cost estimation without a maintenance order in a service order quotation phase.

 

Further references

To know more about the execution order item and its attributes, please refer following blog:

Deep Dive: Execution Order Item and Billable Maintenance Order

entry.title: Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields
entry.content:
Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields

Have you ever wondered how to convert your manually created "old" append fields into real key user custom fields? Doing so lets you easily expose these fields to OData services, SOAP services, SAP GUI transactions, and more, utilizing the same feature set. Have you ever wondered how to do that without losing your data and preserving your old field name, so that you don’t have to adapt your extension coding which consumes this field?

If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you.

Please note that this blog does exclusively address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise and doesn’t address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
In this blog we use S/4HANA as an abbreviation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise. In addition, for key user custom fields we use the abbreviation custom fields.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • In which cases you can convert your manually created append fields and
  • How to convert your manually created append fields to custom fields

Custom field technology supports the conversion of manually created append fields to custom fields using transaction SCFD_EUI. These types of custom fields are called ABAP Managed Fields (abbreviation AMF).
This is especially helpful in the following two cases:

  1. You already have a manually created append in the application database table. Due to the high migration effort needed otherwise, you need to keep this field. However, you also have the requirement to bring this field into Fiori UIs, APIs, or analytical applications using the Custom Fields Fiori app.
  2. You have specific requirements regarding the data element of your custom field, which is not supported when using the Custom Fields Fiori app:
    1. Either you need a specific conversion exit on that field or
    2. You need to use a specific check table, which is then used in SAP GUI transactions, for example

Please note that whenever possible, you should use standard custom fields instead of ABAP Managed Fields. Using ABAP Managed Fields significantly increases complexity, especially in software logistics. We will dive into the details of this issue later in this blog post.

How do you enable an append field for the Custom Fields app?

Let’s assume you have an old append field ZZVOUCHER_CODE at hand, which has been added to the extension include structure SDSALESDOC_INCL_EEW_PS. This structure is included in database table VBAK.
You need to preserve the data of this field because the effort of migrating data and custom code to a new custom field with a different field name is too high.
First, make sure that you are in your key user development system and client. In this client, the Adaptation Transport Organizer must be set up. Second, check that you’re using the correct apps for transport handling (also refer to note 2660797) to assure a correct setup for custom field creation. Further details regarding the setup are addressed in the blog post Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts.

If everything is ready, open transaction SCFD_EUI. You will see the following selection screen:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: selection screen

In this selection screen, you can filter for database table and field name. An example: enter “VBAK” as database table name and “ZZ*” as database field name. If there are too many fields, you could also enter the entire field name ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
Please note that you get built-in documentation describing the details of transaction SCFD_EUI by clicking the Information button on the top of the transaction.

Transaction SCFD_EUI: resulting list of fields

Now, you see the list of fields that apply to the selection criteria, including your field ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
When you select ZZVOUCHER_CODE, a details section below the field list displays additional information for the selected field. Looking at the Field Status column, you see that the content is different for the listed fields. Whereas the status for field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is Can be Enabled, there are also other values possible. All of these are documented and explained here: Enabling Custom Database Fields for Usage in SAP Fiori Applications.

The next step is to click Enable in the button bar on the top. Now, the pop-up Enable Fields for Use in App Custom Fields and Logic is displayed:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: Enabling an append field

The pop-up allows you to specify the Custom Field ID and other semantics of the custom field (in the screenshot, the value help for the Semantic Type has been selected). Since this needs to be a new custom field and not link to an existing one, we leave the default Enabling Type unchanged.
Note that in transaction SCFD_EUI you can’t influence the prefix of the Custom Field ID, as this must be the customer namespace you configured in the Adaptation Transport Organizer settings. This setting is done via the Prefix field in transaction S_ATO_SETUP.

The field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is character-like and should have a value help assigned. Therefore, we need to change the Semantic Type from the default Text to Code List. Please note that possible semantic types depend on the technical type. In this case, it could also be Email Address, Phone Number or Web Address.

As soon as you select Code List as semantic type, the Value Help View field becomes editable. You can either enter the name of an already existing CDS view or enter the name of new CDS view and select Generate right next to the Value Help View field. Clicking Generate will show a pop-up containing templates for both the CDS Code view and the CDS Text view. You can create the two respective views using ABAP development tools for Eclipse (hereafter abbreviated as ADT), copy the templates, and finally, replace the contained placeholders.

Note that both CDS Code view and  CDS Text view must strictly have the format given within the templates. Also, the CDS Text view must be language-specific. However, the template includes a comment with instructions for alternative actions.

In our case, both CDS views have been prepared in advance: the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as code view and the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as text view. The final data source of these CDS views is the manually created database table ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE. All three artifacts are shown below:

CDS Code View ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V:

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZZVOUCHER_CV'
@search.searchable
@ObjectModel.compositionRoot: true
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass: #CUSTOMIZING
@analytics.dataCategory: #DIMENSION
@ObjectModel.representativeKey: 'Code'
define view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as select from ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE
    association [0..*] to ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as _Text
    on $projection.Code = _Text.Code
{
  @ObjectModel.text.association: '_Text'
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  key cast( code as ZZVOUCHER_CODE ) as Code,
  @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_CHILD]
  _Text
}

 

 

 CDS Text View ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T:

 

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZZVOUCHER_CT'
@search.searchable
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass: #CUSTOMIZING
@ObjectModel.dataCategory: #TEXT
@ObjectModel.representativeKey: 'Code'
define view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as select from ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE
  association [0..*] to ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as _Code
    on $projection.Code = _Code.Code
  association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language
    on $projection.Language = _Language.Language
{
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  key cast( code as ZZVOUCHER_CODE ) as Code,

  @Semantics.language: true
  @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
  //Use this for language-dependent descriptions:
  //key <Name of Language Field in Data Source> as Language,
  //Use this for language-independent descriptions:
  key $session.system_language as Language,
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  @Semantics.text: true
  Description as Description,

  @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_ROOT, #TO_COMPOSITION_PARENT]
  _Code,
  _Language
}

 

Database Table ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE (base table of the views):

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzd_voucher_code {

  key client  : mandt not null;
  key code    : zzvoucher_code not null;
  description : cfd_field_code_description;

}

 

 

After creating these CDS views, enter the name of the Code CDS view and click OK. This action carries out the enabling process. Make sure to have note 3355846 installed to avoid timeouts while enabling.

Upon confirmation of the dialog (pressing F8 or OK), you’re prompted to enter a package and a transport request for the objects, which are being created by the system to enable your field for the SAP Fiori app Custom Fields.

 Transaction SCFD_EUI: package selection

After the enabling process is finished, you can see all artifacts generated during the enabling process using the support transaction SCFD_FIELDVIEWER. There, double-click your new custom field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE in the list on the left side of the screen and then select the Enhancement tab on the right side.

Let’s assume that we also want to expose our field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE in the Fiori app Manage Sales Orders - Version 2.
Open the app and navigate to the details of one of the sales orders. Then, switch into the Adapt UI mode. Select the section (the Basic Data – Order Data section) where you’d like to add your ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE field. Then, right-click and select the “+ Add: Field” option. Within the pop-up, select the “+” button in the upper right corner. As soon as you’ve done that, the Custom Fields app will be started with the context of the app to be extended.

In the Custom Fields app, search for your Voucher Code field and navigate to its details. Since the SAP S/4HANA 2023 release, you’ll get a pop-up asking if you want to enable the field for the correct service. In older releases, you need to click on the User Interfaces tab and within that, select the Services tab. Now, look for the OData entity with the description “Manage Sales Orders - Version 2” and enable the entry for this OData service or OData entity by clicking Enable Usage. Then, click Save and Publish to generate the custom field.

 Enable field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE for Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2”

As soon as this is done, reload the Fiori App Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 and then redo the steps you did before. This time, when you click the “+” button, you’ll see the Voucher Code field in the list of available fields. Select the Voucher Code field and click Ok.

In case there are too many fields displayed, you can enter the field name in the search field at the beginning of the dialog.

 

Add “Voucher Code” field to Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2” using Adapt UI

Activate this UI change by selecting the magic wand icon on the upper left corner.
Leave the Adapt UI mode and switch into Edit mode. Now, you’ll see the Voucher Code field on the UI, rendered as field with value help.

 Using the “Voucher Code” in the Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2”

Are ABAP Managed Fields lifecycle-stable, and do they follow the clean core principles? These questions are addressed in the blog post Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts (available soon).

Now, you are done! Congratulations!
With this, you’re now equipped to

  • convert a manually created append field to a custom field using transaction SCFD_EUI
  • know in which cases this is the right thing to do.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts. I want to thank Yasmina van Rooij (YasminavanRooij), Daniel Wachs (DanielWachs), and Georg Wilhelm (georg_wilhelm) for their extremely helpful contributions.
